title: Living in Boulder vlog 1 -  7  - 2017: Google Earthing Rome

enrichment.summary: Kyle is recreating ancient Rome in Google Earth, building 3D structures like the Colosseum, Pantheon, and Temple of Claudius by stacking color-coded height layers. He reflects on the uncanny emotional resonance of virtually inhabiting Roman viewsheds that millions of historical people shared. He also mentions hovering at the edge of financial trouble while living in Boulder.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.topic: Google Earth as a reconstruction tool
stances.position: capable enough to produce convincing ancient Rome if you work within its height-layer constraints
deeply moving — taps into a collective historical psyche shared by millions over centuries
precarious but hopeful he can monetize the project before going into credit card debt

claims.claim: I want to travel around the entire city of Rome like I live there.

transcript.text: Hello, Living in Boulder V-Log. It's 4.57 a.m. on Monday, January 8th.
So I can't remember the last time I did a video. It was five days ago or something.
So I can't remember what I was doing.
I think back in those days, I was doing, like, I had the idea of doing a region a week.
But then after that, I just decided to do a region a day.
And Google Earth in Rome.
So, and then I, because I broke Rome up into all the different regions, the different files on Google Earth.
And then I worked on them individually.
But then today, I saved them together as one file again.
Just because, so I could look at it all at once.
Well, I put them all to look at it all at once, and I saved it.
But, yeah.
So they're all kind of more or less at the same level.
Now, some are better, but, so right now I'm attaching colors, you know, and heights.
I'm, like, doing the finishing touches on all the buildings, making them look realistic.
When I first saw Rome Reborn, if you type Rome Reborn on YouTube, they have these flyovers of ancient Rome, I think in, like, 320 AD.
But when you see it with them, at first I was thinking to myself, oh, wow, that's beyond what YouTube, I mean, Google Earth can do.
Because you can't start, you can't have blocks of color start at a certain height and then end at another height.
And then, you know, taper it with the heights up and down perfectly.
You know, like, if it's a roof, it has to, with Google Earth, it follows the contour of the Earth, so you're limited.
But I was realizing today that you can actually, you can actually do enough with Google Earth, make it look really good.
I was building the Coliseum, and I'm not done yet, but I was in the Coliseum.
It has a colonnade going around it, you know, like a modern Coliseum.
And on Google Earth, I was traveling through it, through the colonnade on the third level of the Coliseum.
And then turning and looking at the view.
So, yeah, that's pretty cool.
You can pretty much do everything.
I built this statue of Nero today.
All you do is you make, you don't make them, you just make the levels, but they hover at a level.
So you just make them in meter increments.
And if you look at it from afar, you know, if you make it solid, it looks like one solid mass.
So it works good enough.
I did the dome of the Pantheon like that.
I'm too lazy to do the screen capture and show to prove.
Because I'm really not that far away from making it look really good anyway.
Like, every day, you know, it's a bunch of new buildings.
So just keep going.
And, yeah, I'm hovering.
I'm still hovering above surface level financially.
So I'll just wait.
Hopefully, hopefully I'll get mixed up making some money on Patreon before I crash.
But I can go into credit card debt for a bit, you know.
I can see myself getting there, getting it to a point where it's...
The thing is, I want to get finished, go go through the room, and then I want to do some videos.
But at that point, I'm probably going to want to polish my website somewhere, the surface stuff.
But it's, yeah, fascinating.
Because today I was, you know, I was just exploring around buildings, and I was coming out the front end of the Temple of Claudius with the Colosseum there.
And I was thinking to myself, oh, my God, that looks like a familiar, what a beautiful sight, you know.
That's the sight that the Romans had.
And, you know, I had those even past life memories where you're like, I know this view.
You know, it's a view that everybody has had.
So many people have had for so many years, hundreds of years, you know, thousands of people, millions of people,
That it had to have been ingrained in the public mind psyche, Akashic Records, that mind.
So when you're looking at the view again on Google Earth, you're hooking up to it, and you're going, oh, my God.
And then you're, so then you're absorbing the world, all of the world of Rome.
And then, and then you, so I want to do that for this whole city.
I want to travel around the entire city of ancient Rome like it's, you know, like I live there.
Because I've been living there for so long.
But there's, you know, there's one thing that was confusing me today.
It's next to the Temple of Claudius.
I should get on a historical forum.
I think I will actually tomorrow and ask this, because I'm super curious.
There's this, there's this track, like a, you know, like a Circus Maximus elliptical track right next to it
That doesn't show up in any of the other maps.
So, and when I look at the name of it, it was like Ludo, Gallica.
It didn't show up.
So, yeah, I think I'm going to go and look at that tomorrow.
Anyways, I think I have babbled long enough.
Okay.
Good night.
